State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660            322-5991           322-6441            322-6444

November 17, 1980

Dunham B. Sherer
Atkinson & Parasyn
P.O. Box 279
Mountain View, CA  94042

Dear Mr. Sherer:

     This letter is in response to your letter of August 13,
1980, and our telephone conversation of October 23, 1980.

     The facts as I understand them are as follows: You are
Deputy Town Attorney for the Town of Woodside. The Town's
Conflict of Interest Code designates your position and requires
that you disclose, among other things, all your investments
that are within the jurisdiction. You, and your immediate
family, own over $1,000 of common stock in Dow-Jones & Co.
Dow-Jones & Co. owns the Wall Street Journal which is sold
in the Town of Woodside. The question you have asked is
whether you must report your investment in Dow-Jones & Co.

     Woodside's Conflict of Interest Code requires that you
report all investments. Government Code Section 82034 provides
that an investment includes ownership of common stock in
excess of $1,000 in a business entity if the business entity
or any of its subsidiaries does business in the jurisdiction.
Dow-Jones & Co. is doing business within the jurisdiction
because its subsidiary the Wall Street Journal sells it
product (newspapers) in Woodside. Therefore, pursuant to
the Town's Conflict of Interest Code, which requires that
you disclose all your investments that are in excess of
$1,000, you must report your interests in Dow-Jones & Co.

     Government Code Section 87302 provides that the Town's
Conflict of Interest Code should set forth, for the purposes
of disclosure by a designated employee, only those types of
investments that can be affected materially by any decision
in which the designated employee participates. If you feel
that the Town's Conflict of Interest Code disclosure require-
ments for your position are too broad, and do not comply
with Government Code Section 87302, you have the right,
pursuant to Government Code Section 87307, to petition the

82035
82034
87310

Dunham B. Sherer
November 17, 1980
Page Two


Town to amend its Code by narrowing the categories of dis-
closure. However, if the disclosure categories that you outline
are so broad or so indelible that it is impossible to draft
narrower disclosure categories, it may require you to disclose
all investments, sources of income, and interests in real
property that are within the jurisdiction pursuant to Government
Code Section 8314.

I hope that this letter is helpful. Please do not
hesitate to contact me if you have any questions regarding
this matter.

Sincerely,


[illegible] Dickerson
General Counsel
Legal Division

DUNHAM

ATKINSON · FARASYN
PAUL B. SMITH
ERIC L. FARASYN
DUNHAM B. SHERER
LEONARD I. SIEGAL
HAROLD S. TOPPEL
FRANK E. MAYO

ATTORNEYS AT LAW
660 WEST DANA STREET
P. O. BOX 279
MOUNTAIN VIEW, CALIFORNIA 94042
(415) 967-6641

Aug 14 8 42 AM '80

August 13, 1980

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re:  Request for Written Advice on Disclosure
     Requirements

Dear Sirs:

     This is a request for written advice, under Government
Code §83114(b), as to my disclosure duties under Title 9 of the
Government Code.

     I am the Deputy Town Attorney for the Town of Woodside.
The scope of my duties is the same as that of the Town Attorney,
since I act for him in his absence. For purposes of this request
then, the appropriate scope of disclosure for my designated
position is the same as that for the Town Attorney.

     My spouse, dependent children and I collectively own
common stock of Dow-Jones & Co., aggregating over $1,000 in value.
The company is listed on the New York Stock Exchange.  Dow-Jones
publishes and sells, among other things, the Wall Street Journal.
The population of the Town of Woodside includes individuals who
subscribe to the Wall Street Journal.

     The national circulation of the Wall Street Journal is
approximately 1,900,000.  There are approximately 2,000 residences
in the Town of Woodside (and a total population of about 5,000).
The circulation of the Wall Street Journal within Woodside is
unknown to me.  A one-year subscription to the Wall Street Journal
costs $63.00.

     Dow-Jones carries on no activities within the Town of
Woodside other than the selling of its publications, and sales of
those publications other than the Wall Street Journal can be
assumed to be insignificant in comparison.

     Under these circumstances, can the common stock of
Dow-Jones & Co. be excluded from the scope of my require disclosure
of economic interests?

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
August 12, 1980
Page Two


If you require additional information, please let
me know.

Very truly yours,

Dunham B. Sherer

DBS/d

ATKINSON · FARASYN
PAUL B. SMITH
ERIC L. FARASYN
DUNHAM B. SHERER
LEONARD J. SIEGAL
HAROLD S. TOPPEL
FRANK E. MAYO

ATTORNEYS AT LAW
660 WEST DANA STREET
P. O. BOX 279

MOUNTAIN VIEW, CALIFORNIA 94042
(415) 967-6941

J.M. ATKINSON, (RETIRED)
J.L.M. FARASYN, (1915-1979)

October 10, 1980


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attention: Technical Assistance

Re: Request for Written Advice on Disclosure
     Requirements

Dear Sirs:

     On August 13, 1980, I sent to you a request for
written advice regarding a specific aspect of my disclosure
obligations for my economic interests. It is my understanding
that under Government Code §83114(b), a written advice should
have been furnished within twenty-one (21) days.

     Certainly more than twenty-one days must have passed
since your receipt of my request. I attach a copy of my original
letter for your information. Please let me know when I may expect
an answer.

                                Very truly yours,

                                [signature]
                                Dunham B. Sherer

DBS/d

Enc.

cc: Robert E. Myers

August 13, 1980


State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95304

                    Re:  Request for Written Advice on Disclosure
                         Requirements

Dear Sirs:

     This is a request for written advice, under Government
Code §83114(b), as to my disclosure duties under Title 9 of the
Government Code.

     I am the Deputy Town Attorney for the Town of Woodside.
The scope of my duties is the same as that of the Town Attorney,
since I act for him in his absence. For purposes of this request
then, the appropriate scope of disclosure for my designated
position is the same as that for the Town Attorney.

     My spouse, dependent children and I collectively own
common stock of Dow-Jones & Co., aggregating over $1,000 in value.
The company is listed on the New York Stock Exchange.  Dow-Jones
publishes and sells, among other things, the Wall Street Journal.
The population of the Town of Woodside includes individuals who
subscribe to the Wall Street Journal.

     The national circulation of the Wall Street Journal is
approximately 1,900,000.  There are approximately 2,000 residences
in the Town of Woodside (and a total population of about 5,000).
The circulation of the Wall Street Journal within Woodside is
unknown to me.  A one-year subscription to the Wall Street Journal
costs $63.00.

     Dow-Jones carries on no activities within the Town of
Woodside other than the selling of its publications, and sales of
those publications other than the Wall Street Journal can be
assumed to be insignificant in comparison.

     Under these circumstances, can the common stock of
Dow-Jones & Co. be excluded from the scope of my require disclosure
of economic interests?

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
August 12, 1980
Page Two


If you require additional information, please let
me know.

Very truly yours,


Dunham B. Sherer

Box 279 Mountain View, CA 94042 Dear Mr. Sherer: This letter is in response to your letter of August 13, 1980, and our telephone conversation of October 23, 1980. The facts as I understand them are as follows. You are Deputy Town Attorney for the Town of Woodside. The Town's Conflict of Interest Code designates your position and requires that you disclose, among other things, all your investments that are within the jurisdiction. You, and your immediate family, own over $1,000 of common stock in Dow-Jones & Co. Dow-Jones & Co. owns the Wall Street Journal which is sold in the Town of Woodside. The question you have asked is whether you must report your investment in Dow-Jones & Co. Woodside's Conflict of Interest Code requires that you report all investments. Government Code Section 82034 provides that an investment includes ownership of common stock in excess of $1,000 in a business entity if the business entity or any of its subsidiaries does business in the jurisdiction. Dow-Jones & Co. is doing business within the jurisdiction because its subsidiary the Wall Street Journal sells its product (newspapers) in Woodside. Therefore, pursuant to the Town's Conflict of Interest Code, which requires that you disclose all your investments that are in excess of $1,000, you must report your interests in Dow-Jones & Co. Government Code Section 87302 provides that the Town's Conflict of Interest Code should set forth, for the purposes of disclosure by a designated employee, only those types of investments that can be affected materially by any decision in which the designated employee participates. If you feel that the Town's Conflict of Interest Code disclosure requirements for your position are too broad, and do not comply with Government Code Section 87302, you have the right, pursuant to Government Code Section 87307, to petition the Town to amend its Code by narrowing the categories of disclosure. However, if the Town determines that your duties are so broad or indefinable that it is impossible to draft narrower disclosure categories, it may require you to disclose all investments, sources of income, and interests in real property that are within the jurisdiction pursuant to Government Code Section 87315. I hope that this letter is helpful. Please do not hesitate to contact me, if you have any questions regarding this matter. Sincerely, W. E.
